retrieved_docs.title: Cultural Competence in Caring for American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Consequently, when designing preventive programs to reduce the prevalence of obesity, diabetes, and CVD among adults and children in the AI/AN community, it is imperative to consider various factors, including cultural, economic, and regional aspects. [12] Prevention programs should focus on the following key elements: Addressing behavioral risk factors and promoting lifestyle improvements Encouraging tobacco avoidance Promoting a balanced diet Increasing physical activity levels Significant evidence links tobacco misuse, high cholesterol levels, DM, and hypertension to the increased incidence of CVDs in the AI/AN population. [13] An analysis of SHS outcome data pertaining to chronic heart disease (CHD) has highlighted the following significant risk factors for CHD within this population: [14] [15] Age Gender Total cholesterol Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol High-density lipoprotein cholesterol Smoking Albuminuria DM High blood pressure

retrieved_docs.title: Cultural Competence in Caring for American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: AI/AN patients exhibit a lower life expectancy than most other ethnic groups. Compared to white individuals, AI/AN groups are also more likely to face limited opportunities for pursuing higher education, which can increase their vulnerability to various detrimental chronic conditions and lower quality of life. A survey-based analysis indicates that AI/AN individuals are inclined to consume more sugar-sweetened beverages, which can contribute to obesity and subsequently predispose them to hypertension and diabetes. This population has also reported a sedentary lifestyle and limited physical activity in residential settings, leading to poor health outcomes. The lack of higher education creates a barrier for this group to access high-paying jobs, which results in unemployment and impedes progress. These factors necessitate immediate action to enhance preventive measures to address chronic conditions within the population and mitigate social inequities. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Cultural Competence in Caring for American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: During interviews conducted through the BRFSS, it was found that AI adults residing in the 7 Montanna reservations had a significantly high level of modifiable risk factors for CVD, both with and without DM. Prominent risk factors for DM and CVD include physical inactivity, obesity, and hypertension. Physical inactivity is considered a modifiable risk factor, and obesity is a controllable risk factor. AI/AN individuals can manage DM, CVD, and obesity through regular exercise. Engaging in exercise and achieving weight loss are recognized strategies for reducing blood pressure, improving insulin sensitivity, preventing or delaying the onset of type 2 diabetes, lowering the risk of CVD, and reducing the likelihood of heart attacks and strokes. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention Strategies -- Definition/Introduction -- Primordial Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Many areas in continental Africa have significantly increased educational measures towards primary prevention. There is a significant difference between countries that receive preventive education on specific diseases and those that do not. Education of children on key topics, such as diet, exercise, and common diseases, has been statistically shown to help improve cardiovascular health in younger populations and reduce the risk of developing more serious illnesses later in life. These findings demonstrate the potential impact of childhood preventive education.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation and Management of Perioperative Hypertension -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: It has long been taught that BP increases as a normal consequence of aging. Recent work indicates this may not be so. The Yanomami, who live in the Amazon rainforest and ingest minimal salt, have the lowest BP of any society in the world. [5] In contrast, the Yekwana, who live nearby and have had some exposure to salt, have higher levels of BP, albeit far lower than modern societies. [5] Therefore, HTN may be a result of salt intake and/or a modern lifestyle. Additional risk factors for HTN include family history, increased weight, sedentary lifestyle, cigarette smoking, and a diet low in potassium.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiologic and Etiologic Considerations of Obesity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Obesity is a complex, multifactorial condition and one of the leading preventable causes of morbidity and mortality, with a profound societal impact. Driven by genetic, socioeconomic, dietary, and lifestyle factors, obesity's prevalence has risen with modern shifts toward caloric excess and sedentary behavior. Additional contributors include racial and socioeconomic disparities, prenatal influences, and environmental changes promoting processed food consumption and decreased physical activity. Obesity is associated with significant comorbidities, including metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and hypertension, and can be exacerbated by conditions like hypothyroidism, Cushing syndrome, and certain medications, creating a need for comprehensive, personalized approaches to management.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Prevention -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors important in stroke prevention can be divided into non-modifiable and modifiable risk factors. [3] [4] [5] Non-modifiable risk factors include Age (increased risk with increasing age) Race and ethnicity (higher risk of strokes in Africans than whites) Gender (higher risk for men, but this trend disappears beyond the mid-80s) Family history (especially of cerebral arterial disease) Genetic risk factors (for example, hypercoagulable states, sickle cell disease, cerebral amyloid angiopathy) Modifiable risk factors Hypertension Diabetes mellitus Obesity Impaired lipid profile Atrial fibrillation Structural heart disease Carotid artery stenosis Lifestyle choices- Diet, Exercise, Smoking, Alcohol, etc. In most cases, the patient has a combination of modifiable risk factors, i.e., hypertension, diabetes, high cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Life Expectancy and Overall Health
retrieved_docs.content: In 2018, 17.4% AIAN population reported being in fair or poor health compared to 10% of the NHW population.

retrieved_docs.title: Cultural Competence in Caring for American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the unique cultural and historical factors that influence the healthcare experiences and outcomes of American Indian/Alaska Native patients. Screen for the prevalence of chronic conditions, including diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and mental health disorders, among American Indian/Alaska Native patients and understand their impact on health outcomes. Implement culturally competent preventive measures, such as nutritionist referrals, blood glucose monitoring, and early screening for hypertension and cancer, to address specific health needs for American Indian/Alaska Native patients. Coordinate care with interdisciplinary healthcare teams, including traditional healers, to ensure holistic and culturally competent care plans for American Indian/Alaska Native patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cultural Competence in Caring for American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cultural competency is prioritized when providing care to American Indian/Alaska Native (AI/AN) patients due to the higher likelihood of increased mortality rates within this population, as they are more frequently affected by type 1 diabetes and other chronic disorders. Primary care physicians must implement preventive measures, including early interventions such as referring patients to nutritionists, conducting regular blood glucose checks, and initiating early screening for chronic uncontrolled hypertension, renal disease, cardiovascular diseases, and cancer. This activity reviews the clinical and public health challenges experienced by AI/AN patients, emphasizing the crucial role of interprofessional teams in evaluating and treating these conditions and addressing associated adversities.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Etiology -- Demographic Differences
retrieved_docs.content: The stark prevalence of poverty, lack of education, and higher unemployment rates in the AIAN community also add to healthcare disparity. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance -- Mortality Disparity Rates
retrieved_docs.content: There are devastatingly high death rates among AIAN adults as well. Compared to the general United States population, AIAN adults continue to die at higher rates from several conditions. [4] [61] These include: Heart disease Accidents/unintentional injuries (eg, motor vehicle injuries) Diabetes mellitus Alcohol consumption Chronic lower respiratory disease Cerebrovascular disease (stroke) Chronic liver disease and cirrhosis Influenza and pneumonia Nephritis, nephrotic syndrome Intentional self-harm/suicide Septicemia Assault (homicide) Essential hypertension diseases

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Issues of Concern -- Historical & Sociopolitical Context
retrieved_docs.content: The forced relocation to reservations and seizure of land and natural resources restricted access to traditional food sources through hunting, gathering, fishing, and farming. AIAN communities became increasingly reliant on the Food Distribution Program on Indian Reservations. This program, which the U.S. Department of Agriculture developed, provides canned and packed food high in sugar and fat. [13] These nutrient-deficient meals have been associated with the high prevalence of obesity, diabetes, and hypertension seen amongst AIANs today. [14] [8] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Chronic Health Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: 17.4 percent of AIAN reported having fair or poor health compared with 8.3 percent of NHW. AIAN have a rate of HIV infection twice as high as that of NHW In 2017, AIAN adults were almost three times more likely to have diabetes and 2.5 times more likely to die from the same than NHW adults. AIAN adolescents are 30 percent more likely than NHW adolescents to be obese. Tobacco Consumption [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2754
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension, a common form of high blood pressure, is interpreted by the term ضغط الدم, the Arabic phrase that directly translates to “blood pressure,” highlighting the linguistic link between the clinical condition and its description.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1710
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the two conditions share comparable signs, symptoms, and pathophysiological features. The phrase hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension is interpreted in Arabic as “ضغط الدم,” which directly translates to blood pressure. Recognizing that malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis can mimic severe or complicated hypertension underscores the importance of careful evaluation of blood‑pressure–related organ damage and the need for targeted therapeutic strategies in patients presenting with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a primary form of high blood pressure that frequently presents with an elevated mean arterial pressure as a clinical manifestation. Hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) is closely linked to essential hypertension, indicating that severe acute blood‑pressure elevations can arise in patients with this condition. Essential hypertension also has a recognized relationship with metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض), reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms and overlapping risk factors. Moreover, essential hypertension is commonly mapped to systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقبالي), emphasizing that the systolic component often predominates in its presentation. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians identify the characteristic signs, potential complications, and associated comorbidities of essential hypertension, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is clinically associated with several conditions and findings. Patients with essential hypertension often exhibit depressive disorders, may have abnormal chest X‑ray results, and can experience tremor. In addition, essential hypertension corresponds to both diastolic hypertension and systolic hypertension, reflecting its classification into the two primary components of elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these associations and the dual nature of essential hypertension aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2104
retrieved_docs.content: The vascular domain encompasses several distinct forms of elevated blood pressure. Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated mean arterial pressure are recognized as specific vascular abnormalities that reflect increased pressure in the circulatory system. Within the same category, the Arabic‑named conditions ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) are also classified as vascular children. Together, these entities illustrate the range of hypertensive disorders that fall under the broader vascular classification, highlighting the importance of identifying and managing each type to maintain optimal cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is characterized by hemodynamic abnormalities such as elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure, both of which are direct manifestations of the condition. In clinical practice, essential hypertension can progress to a hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), a severe escalation that is mapped to the underlying essential hypertension. Additionally, essential hypertension is clinically associated with gout (مرض النقرس), indicating that patients with this form of high blood pressure have an increased likelihood of developing or co‑existing with gout. Recognizing these interrelated features—blood pressure elevations, the potential for crisis, and the link to gout—helps clinicians assess risk, monitor disease progression, and tailor comprehensive management strategies for affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1844
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension, also identified by the Arabic term ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ), is defined by blood pressure values that are above the reference range, representing an increased measurement. This “above reference range” finding is classified as a type of increased result and is interpreted as essential hypertension. Clinically, benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis is considered similar to essential hypertension, reflecting overlapping pathophysiological features between primary hypertension and arteriolar kidney disease. The inclusion of ارتفاع ضغط الدم within the essential hypertension SMQ underscores its role as a primary hypertensive disorder, and recognizing its relationship to increased blood‑pressure readings and related renal conditions is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2554
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis is classified as a subtype of اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder) and shares clinical characteristics with essential hypertension, indicating that patients with this form of arteriolar kidney disease often present with blood‑pressure patterns and target‑organ effects similar to those seen in essential hypertension. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertensive disease from secondary renal vascular pathology and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1051
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a specific circulatory system disease that arises during gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period. In Arabic, this condition is expressed by the term ضغط الدم, which directly translates to “blood pressure.” It is recognized as a subclass of the broader category Disease of circulatory system complicating pregnancy childbirth and puerperium, placing it within the spectrum of maternal circulatory disorders. Clinically, it is classified under the heading hypertension complicating pregnancy; childbirth and the puerperium, reflecting its role as a hypertensive disorder unique to the maternal timeline. Understanding this terminology and classification helps clinicians identify and manage blood‑pressure abnormalities that can impact both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2859
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are vascular conditions that belong to the broader Vascular category. Within this classification, the Arabic terms فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي correspond to the same entities of diastolic and systolic hypertension, respectively. Recognizing these entities as children of Vascular underscores that they are specific subtypes of hypertension affecting the circulatory system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1994
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, and the term essential hypertension is interpreted in Arabic as “ضغط الدم” (blood pressure). Hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension is likewise interpreted by the Arabic phrase “ضغط الدم”. Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension. Together, these relationships highlight that both benign and malignant forms of arteriolar nephrosclerosis share clinical features with different categories of hypertension, and that the Arabic term “ضغط الدم” is used to denote both essential hypertension and hypertension with complications or secondary causes, underscoring the linguistic and clinical connections among these vascular and renal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2929
retrieved_docs.content: Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten, a category of other cerebrovascular diseases, includes as a subcategory the condition denoted in Arabic as اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم, which describes a brain disorder caused by excess blood pressure. This relationship underscores that hypertension can give rise to specific cerebrovascular pathology within the broader group of other cerebrovascular diseases.
